segments.s466-g1: دبیر جهاندیده را is a definite direct object marked by را (object‑marker) and placed before the verb, a common literary order. پیش خواند is a past tense verbal phrase: خواند (3sg past) with پیش ‘before/in the presence of’, so خواند here means ‘called/summoned (into his presence)’. The whole segment reads ‘he summoned the worldly‑wise scribe (into his presence)’.
segments.s466-g2: This string lists two further past actions. بیاورد is a past narrative form ‘(he) brought (him)’, نزدیک qualifies location ‘near’, گاهش is the possessive/compound (گاه + -ش) meaning something like ‘his place/seat’ (poetic use; no exact sense provided), and نشاند is the transitive past of ‘to seat (someone)’ (archaic literary form). Together: ‘(he) brought him and seated him near his place.’
segments.s466-g3: یکی نامه بنوشت is a literary order where یکی (‘one’/‘a certain’) modifies نامه and precedes the verb; بنوشت is an archaic/perfective form meaning ‘wrote’. با داغ و درد is an instrumental/manner phrase ‘with (burning) grief and pain’ describing the state in which the letter was written.
segments.s466-g4: This is a concise descriptive clause: دو دیده ‘two eyes’ is the subject, پر از خون ‘full of blood’ uses پر + از to mean ‘full of’, and رخ لاژورد is a noun + modifier where لاژورد (lapis/ultramarine) describes the face’s color/appearance. There is no copula; the phrase describes his bloody eyes and a lapis‑blue face.
segments.s466-g5: ز (poetic از) introduces origin ‘from’. دارای here functions as an epithet/title attached to the name داراب (Darab); بن (in this text functions as the Arabic patronymic ‘son of’, though that specific sense is not in the provided list) links the name to اردشیر (Ardashir). Together the phrase names the sender: ‘from the lord Darab, son of Ardashir.’
segments.s466-g6: سوی is the preposition ‘toward/to’. قیصر اسکندر is a title + name (‘Caesar Alexander’), and شهرگیر is a postpositive epithet ‘city‑capturer’ (an epithet of conquest). The whole segment indicates the addressee: ‘to Caesar Alexander, the conqueror of cities.’
segments.s466-g7: نخست is an adverb ‘first/firstly’. آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction: آفرین (praise/acclamation) + کرد (‘made/did’) = ‘he offered praise’ or ‘he praised’. بر کردگار is the prepositional object ‘to/upon the Creator’ (کردگار = God). The structure is ‘First he praised the Creator.’
segments.s466-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘for/since/that’). زو (written here as ز + و) functions as ‘from him’ (ز = from, و representing the pronoun clitic ‘-u/او’ in older spelling). دید is 3sg past ‘he saw/was shown’, and نیک و بد روزگار is the object phrase ‘the good and bad of fate/fortune’. So: ‘for from Him he had seen the good and bad of fortune.’
segments.s466-g41: همه به اصفهانند = literal word order places the locative phrase before the copula: 'همه' (all/everyone) + 'به اصفهان' (to/in Isfahan) + 'ـند' (3rd pl. copula). 'بی‌درد و رنج' is a coordinated adjectival phrase describing their state (carefree, without suffering). This line uses classical/poetic word order (predicate parts separated) rather than modern spoken order.
segments.s466-g42: ازیشان مبادا که خواهیم گنج = 'از ایشان' (from/of them) + 'مبادا' (optative/negative: 'may it not be' / 'God forbid') introduces a wish/negation clause. 'که' introduces the subordinate clause and 'خواهیم' is 1st plural present/future sense 'we will/wish', followed by the noun 'گنج' (treasure). Overall: a negative optative construction meaning 'may it not be that we want/seek treasure from them.'
segments.s466-g43: تو‌گر = conditional 'تو گر' (if you). 'سوی ایران' is a directional phrase 'toward/into Iran.' 'خرامی' is a poetic/archaic present participial/adverbial form meaning 'striding proudly' (describing how you go). 'رواست' is 'روا' (fitting/permissible) + the copula 'ست' (it is), so the clause means 'it is fitting.'
segments.s466-g44: همه پادشاهی سراسر تراست = 'همه پادشاهی سراسر' ('the whole kingdom throughout') is the nominal subject phrase; 'تراست' is a fused copular/possessive expression meaning 'is yours' (تَ + را + است or idiomatic 'it belongs to you'). The predicate states that the entire kingship belongs to you.
segments.s466-g45: ز فرمان تو یک زمان نگذریم = 'ز' (poetic 'from/of') + 'فرمان تو' ('your command') sets an ablative/source phrase. 'یک زمان' = 'a single moment' (emphatic time unit). 'نگذریم' is 1st plural negative present/subjunctive/optative 'we shall not pass/depart' used here to mean 'we will not go a moment without' or 'we will not neglect.' The verb ends the clause in classical order.
segments.s466-g46: نفس نیز بی‌راه تو نشمریم = 'نفس نیز' ('not even a breath' / 'even the breath') uses 'نیز' as 'even/also' for emphasis. 'بی‌راه تو' is an adjectival phrase 'away from/astray from you' (بی‌راه = wrong/off the path + تو = you). 'نشمریم' is 1st person plural negative subjunctive/present of 'شماریدن' ('to count/consider'): 'we will not count/consider [it so].' Together: 'nor will we count even a breath as estraying from you.'
segments.s466-g47: بکردار کشتی بیامد هیون = 'بکردار' ('of good conduct / of noble bearing') is an adjective placed before the noun it modifies. The verb 'بیامد' (past 'came') follows the object 'کشتی' (ship) for poetic inversion, and 'هیون' (beast/creature) is the subject placed after the verb — idiomatic poetic word order: 'a beast of noble bearing came to the ship.'
segments.s466-g48: دل و دیده تاجور پر ز خون = 'دل و دیده' (heart and eye[s]) form a coordinated possessed phrase; 'تاجور' follows them as the possessor (Tajur, here treated as a named person/merchant). 'پر ز خون' = 'full of blood' with 'پر' (full) and the ablative 'ز' ('of/from') plus 'خون' (blood). Word order places possessor after the possessed phrase, a common Persian poetic structure.
segments.s466-g25: کسی را که داری is a relative clause meaning “someone whom you have.” کسی را is the definite/object-marked noun phrase ('someone' with را marking it as the object of داری). که introduces the relative clause; داری is 2nd-person singular present ‘you have/hold.’ ز پیوند من follows as a prepositional phrase: ز (poetic from) + پیوند (bond/kinship) + من (my), giving “from my kin/ties.”
segments.s466-g26: This continues and narrows the source: ز governs both following nouns. پوشیده‌رویان is plural and here refers to the veiled/modest-faced people; و links that group to فرزند من (‘and my child/offspring’). The whole phrase means “from my veiled-faced (people) and my children.”
segments.s466-g27: بر من فرستی is literal word order “(you) send against me”: بر (against/on) + من (me) + فرستی (you send — 2nd-person present). نباشد شگفت is an impersonal poetic negative: نباشد (“there is not / it would not be”) + شگفت (wonder), so together “there would be no wonder / it would not be surprising.”
segments.s466-g28: جهانجوی را marks the person (the ambitious/world‑seeker) with the object marker را. کین نباید گرفت is an impersonal modal construction: نباید (‘one should not’) + گرفت (take/seize), with کین (‘vengeance, hatred’) as the object — “one should not take vengeance on a world‑seeker.”
segments.s466-g29: ز پوشیده‌رویان = “from/among the veiled-faced (people).” به جز سرزنش is an excluding phrase: به جز (‘except’) + سرزنش (‘reproach’), so the clause says that from those veiled-faced ones there is nothing except reproach.
segments.s466-g30: نباشد is the impersonal negative ‘there is not / there would not be.’ ز شاهان = ‘from/among the kings.’ برتر منش puts the comparative adjective before the noun (برتر = superior, منش = character), so the phrase means “there is no superior character among kings.”
segments.s466-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ نامه بخواند is a classical present/subjunctive form meaning ‘reads (the) letter’ (بخواند = he reads). خداوند is the subject ‘the sovereign/lord.’ هوش (placed after the subject for emphasis) is the object meaning ‘awareness/wit’ — the clause means ‘when the sovereign reads the letter, (he) will rouse his wits.’
segments.s466-g32: بیاراید is a 3rd-person verb meaning ‘he will bring/arrange/adorn’ (here: cause to come forth/present). این رای is the direct object ‘this counsel/plan.’ پاسخ‌نیوش is a compound noun ‘answer‑listener’ / ‘recipient of the reply’; the whole means that he will present or deliver this counsel to the reply‑recipient (i.e. it will be put before the one who hears/receives replies).
segments.s466-g33: هیونی (a wild animal) is the subject placed at the start for emphasis. ز کرمان literally 'from Kerman' is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = from) marking origin. بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came' (3rd person singular). دوان is an adverbial participle meaning 'running' or 'in haste' and follows the verb here to indicate manner; the word order (subject — origin — verb — manner) is a poetic inversion but grammatically straightforward.
segments.s466-g34: به نزدیک = a prepositional phrase 'toward / near' (به + نزدیک) that points the motion toward the following proper name. اسکندر is the proper name 'Alexander' as the target of that motion. بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious / mistrustful' qualifying either the beast as it approaches or Alexander (poetic ambiguity); its postnominal position is typical in older Persian and functions as a predicate/adjective attached to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s466-g35: سکندر is the subject. چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. آن نامه is the object 'that letter'. برخواند is a past tense verb meaning 'read aloud / recited' (the prefix بر- with خواند indicates reading aloud) and governs the temporal clause introduced by چو. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past) that follows the subordinate temporal clause; the sequence 'چو ... برخواند گفت' functions as 'when he read ... , he said'.
segments.s466-g36: که introduces the content clause ('that...'). با is the preposition 'with'. جان here means 'life' (the thing to be paired). دارا in this context is a proper name (Dara/Darius) rather than the lexical adjective 'wealthy', so it is best read as a name. خرد is 'wisdom'. باد is the optative particle 'may' expressing a wish. جفت is a noun 'pair/companion' and closes the clause: 'that with the life of Dara, wisdom may be paired' = 'may wisdom be joined with Dara's life.'
segments.s466-g37: کسی کو is an archaic relative/indefinite construction meaning 'whoever' (کسی + کو). گراید is a present/subjunctive form of 'to turn/ incline' used here to mean 'would attach' or 'be drawn'. به پیوند اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to/into his bond/alliance' where پیوند is 'bond, alliance' and اوی is an archaic possessive 'his'. The clause functions as a protasis ('whoever joins himself to his alliance').
segments.s466-g38: به introduces the object of attachment again 'to' or 'toward'. پوشیده‌رویان is a collective plural 'the veiled-faced / modest ones' and functions as the first item in a coordinated object. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. فرزند is 'child / descendant' and اوی again is the archaic possessive 'his' (referring back to the same possessor). The whole segment lists the groups to whom the bond would be made.
segments.s466-g39: نبیند is a negative present/subjunctive form ('he will not see' / 'he sees not') often used in classical Persian to express an inevitable future result. مگر is 'except / only' (introducing the sole thing he will see). تخته گور is a genitive phrase 'plank/board of the grave' (تخته = board, گور = grave). تخت here is an appositive noun meaning 'bed / bier / platform' (poetically describing the same object), so the whole phrase means nothing will be seen except the grave's plank/platform.
segments.s466-g40: گر is the conditional 'if'. آویخته is a past-participle adjective 'hung' describing the state. سر is 'head'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. شاخ درخت is 'the branch of a tree' (شاخ = bough/branch, درخت = tree). Together this clause gives the lethal outcome: 'if his head is hung from a tree-branch' (i.e., he will be hanged), which connects back as the circumstance producing the grave/plank.
segments.s466-g9: دگر گفت is a short clause meaning 'then/again he said' (دگر here = 'again' or 'then'); گفت is the verb 'said' (past). کز is the subordinating conjunction 'that/which', introducing the content clause. گردش آسمان is a genitive phrase 'the revolution/circuit of the heavens' (گردش = rotation/revolution; آسمان = heaven/sky).
segments.s466-g10: خردمند is the agent/noun 'the wise (one)'. برنگذرد is a negative present/future-like verb form 'does not / will not pass/escape' (بر + نگذرد), so the clause means 'the wise will not escape'; بی‌گمان is a sentence-final adverb 'undoubtedly'.
segments.s466-g11: کزو is an archaic/poetic compound of از + او meaning 'from it / because of it'. شادمانیم is a present-tense first-person-plural predicate 'we are joyful' (شادمان + ایم). و links the clauses; ز here is another reduced form of از ('from/of'), and ناشکیб is a predicate/adjective 'impatient/restless' applied to the same subject 'we'.
segments.s466-g12: گهی ... گهی is a paired adverbial 'sometimes ... sometimes' (repetition gives alternation). در فراز = 'on/in the heights' and در نشیب = 'in the descent/low point' — short locative phrases specifying the alternation just stated.
segments.s466-g13: نه at the start negates the nominal phrase: 'not a ...' or 'no ...'. مردی بد is 'a bad man' (مردی = man), and این رزم ما با سپاه is the noun phrase 'this battle of ours with/against an army' (این = this, رزم = battle, ما = our, با سپاه = with an army). The clause is elliptical: an implied verb like 'can do' or 'succeeds' is omitted, so the sense is 'no bad man can contend in this battle against an army.'
segments.s466-g14: مگر introduces an exception 'except/unless'. بخشش here means 'a grant/favor' (not 'forgiveness' in this context). گردش هور و ماه is a genitive construction 'the turning/revolution of hūr and moon' (هور = radiant light / the sun; ماه = moon), i.e. fortune or the cosmic turns — so the whole phrase means 'except by favor and the turning of sun and moon.'
segments.s466-g15: کنون is the adverb 'now'. بودنی is a verbal/adjectival noun 'to-be/that which is' or 'being', and بود is the past-tense 'was' — together they give a sense like 'now it was to be / now there was being'. و links to the next clause; ما is the subject 'we' and دل به درد is an idiomatic phrase 'heart into pain' meaning 'to have a sorrowful heart' (i.e. 'be grieved').
segments.s466-g16: چه داریم is a rhetorical question 'what do we have / why should we have'; ازین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (از + این) meaning 'from/of this' (ین is the archaic/orthographic form of این). گنبد لاژورد is a descriptive noun phrase 'lapis-lazuli dome' ('گنبد' = dome, 'لاژورد' = lapis lazuli / deep blue stone). The whole clause asks why we should be troubled by this blue dome.
segments.s466-g17: کنون‌گر is an archaic conditional phrase meaning 'if now' (کنون = now, -گر = 'if'); بسازی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form of ساختن ('you build/you would build'); و is the coordinating 'and'; پیمان is a noun 'covenant/pact'; کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used with پیمان to mean 'you make/perform (a pact)'. The whole segment sets up a conditional: 'If now you build and make a pact...'.
segments.s466-g18: دل is the patient noun 'the heart'; از is the preposition 'from/of' marking source or cause; جنگ ایران = 'the war (against) Iran' (جنگ 'war', ایران 'Iran'); پشیمان is an adjective 'regretful/repentant'; کنی is again 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن here meaning 'you cause' (i.e. 'you make [the heart] regret'). Grammatically this is the result clause of the condition: 'you will cause the heart to be sorry about the war with Iran.'
segments.s466-g19: همه is a quantifier 'all'; it modifies گنج 'treasure(s)'; the two proper names گشتاسپ و اسفندیار are conjoined possessors/owners. Classical/poetic Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe here: the phrase reads 'all the treasure of Gushtasp and Esfandiyar.'
segments.s466-g20: همان 'that/the same' points back to the previously listed riches; یاره is 'beloved/companion' used here as a valued person or ally; و is 'and'; تاج گوهرنگار is a noun phrase 'crown (تاج) adorned/engraved with gems' where گوهرنگار functions adjectivally ('jewel‑studded'). Word order (noun + modifier) is typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s466-g21: فرستم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive (used as a promise/future here) of فرستادن 'I will send'; به marks the indirect object 'to/into'; گنج تو 'your treasury' (گنج + the possessive تو); از 'from'; گنج خویش is 'from my own treasure' —خویش is a reflexive/possessive form ('one's own') formed here as خوی + ش suffix. The clause states the action 'I will send (into) your treasure from my treasure...'.
segments.s466-g22: همان نیز = 'the same also/likewise' (linking back to the items just mentioned); ورزیده is an adjective 'experienced/seasoned' or 'well‑tested'; رنج is 'suffering/hardship'; خویش (again split as خوی + ش) is the possessive 'one's own' (here 'my own'). There is no verb in this short clause — it is coordinated with the previous clause, listing additional things the speaker will send.
segments.s466-g23: همان 'that same' picks up what was offered; مر ترا here represents an archaic/clitic sequence meaning roughly 'to you (me)' or idiomatically functions as 'I (for) you' — مر = a form of 'me' (مرا), ت = 2nd‑person clitic, را = the object/topic marker (the tokens are split in the edition). یار is 'ally/companion' (here 'ally'); باشم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of بودن used as a promise 'I will be' or 'I shall be'; به جنگ means 'in battle/at war'. Altogether: 'I will be that same ally to you in battle.'
segments.s466-g24: به‌روز 'by day / day‑time' (literally 'of the day'); و 'and'; شبانت here is a cliticized form 'your nights' or more idiomatically 'night and day' (the edition splits the token); نسازم is the negative 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of ساختن ('I will not make/produce/cause'); درنگ is a noun 'pause/delay'. The clause promises continuous action: 'day and night I will not cause a pause' (i.e. I will not let there be any delay/rest).

sentences.s466-s1: He summoned the worldly‑wise scribe, brought him forward, and seated him near his place.
sentences.s466-s2: He wrote a letter — with burning grief and pain; his two eyes were full of blood and his face was like lapis‑lazuli.
sentences.s466-s3: From the lord Darab, son of Ardashir — to Caesar Alexander, the capturer of cities.
sentences.s466-s4: First he praised the Creator, for from Him he had seen the good and the bad of fortune.
sentences.s466-s21: They are all in Isfahan, without pain or hardship — may it not be that we desire treasure from them.
sentences.s466-s22: If you go into Iran with a proud stride, it is fitting; the whole kingdom throughout is yours.
sentences.s466-s23: We will not, for a single moment, act contrary to your command; nor will we count even a breath as estranged from you.
sentences.s466-s24: A beast boarded the ship of noble bearing; Tajur’s heart and eyes were full of blood.
sentences.s466-s13: Anyone whom you have among my kin — among my veiled-faced (ones) and my children.
sentences.s466-s14: If you send someone against me, it would not be surprising; one should not take vengeance on a world‑seeker.
sentences.s466-s15: From the veiled-faced there is nothing but reproach; among kings there is no superior character.
sentences.s466-s16: When the sovereign reads the letter, he will rouse his wits; he will present this counsel to the recipient of the reply.
sentences.s466-s17: A wild beast from Kerman came running toward Alexander, suspicious.
sentences.s466-s18: When Alexander read that letter aloud, he said:
sentences.s466-s19: Whoever attaches himself to his alliance with the veiled-faced and his offspring...
sentences.s466-s20: he will see nothing but the plank of a grave—if his head hangs from a tree-branch.
sentences.s466-s5: Then he said that the wise surely do not escape the revolutions of the heavens.
sentences.s466-s6: Because of it we are joyful and because of it restless; sometimes on high and sometimes in low.
sentences.s466-s7: No mere bad man can handle this battle of ours against an army, unless by favor and the turning of sun and moon.
sentences.s466-s8: Now it was to be, and why should we trouble our hearts over this lapis‑lazuli dome?
sentences.s466-s9: If now you build and make a covenant, you will cause the heart to regret warring against Iran.
sentences.s466-s10: All the treasures of Gushtasp and Esfandiyar, that same companion and the jewel‑studded crown.
sentences.s466-s11: I will send into your treasury from my own treasure the same things, and likewise my tried (seasoned) hardships.
sentences.s466-s12: I will be that very ally to you in battle; day and night I will allow no pause.
